summary: Spolsky introduces the concepts of 'user model' and 'program model' to explain why software interfaces succeed or fail. Users approach software with preexisting mental models shaped by prior experience, and when the program's actual behavior diverges from these expectations, frustration results. He argues that designers should almost always change the program model to match user expectations rather than trying to educate users. The post provides practical advice on discovering user models through cheap, informal usability testing with just five or six people. He illustrates the principle with examples from FrontPage, Excel's window layering, and Alt+Tab behavior, showing how even Microsoft frequently gets this wrong. The core message is to keep program models simple, because users' mental models are always simpler than developers expect.
key_points: Users bring preexisting mental models ('user model inertia') from prior software experience, and these expectations are nearly impossible to change through documentation or dialog boxes
When the user model and program model conflict, you should almost always change the program to match user expectations rather than trying to educate users
You can discover user models cheaply by asking just 5-6 people what they expect to happen — formal usability labs aren't necessary
User models are never complex: when people guess how software works, they guess simple things, so designers should pick the simplest possible program model
Even major software like Excel and Windows gets this wrong, with invisible window sheets and non-obvious Alt+Tab behavior that violate users' simple mental models
money_quotes: When a new user sits down to use a program, they do not come with a completely clean slate. They have some expectations of how they think the program is going to work.
If the program model corresponds to the user model, you have a successful user interface.
You can try to change the user model. This turns out to be remarkably hard.
So, if the mountain won't come to Muhammad … your best choice is almost always going to be to change the program model, not the user model.
Just ask a bunch of users where they think the thumbnails are going to be stored.
An important rule of thumb is that user models aren't very complex. When people have to guess how a program is going to work, they tend to guess simple things, rather than complicated things.
If your program model is nontrivial, it's probably not the user model.
It's hard enough to make the program model conform to the user model when the models are simple. When the models become complex, it's even more unlikely. So pick the simplest possible model.
key_insight: Successful UI design requires discovering users' preexisting mental models through simple testing and adapting the program's behavior to match those expectations, because users always assume simpler models than developers build.
